title: Iran Closes Hormuz as US Launches Third Round of Strikes, Spain's Wildfire Toll Hits 12, Typhoon Bavi Slams China
subhead: The Strait of Hormuz is officially closed after Iran's IRGC attacked another commercial vessel, triggering a third US strike round. Spain faces one of its deadliest wildfires, and Typhoon Bavi forces nearly two million from their homes in China.

sections.heading: Iran Closes Strait of Hormuz After Attacking Another Vessel
sections.body:
Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) declared the Strait of Hormuz closed until further notice on Sunday after firing a naval cruise missile at a Cyprus-flagged vessel — the MV GFS Galaxy — that was transiting the waterway. According to the BBC, the IRGC said the ship was hit after it turned off its tracking systems and diverted from an approved route. US Central Command (Centcom) said the crew was forced to abandon ship into a lifeboat, with one crew member missing and significant damage to the engine room.

The attack triggered what Centcom described as the 'third round of strikes this week' on Iranian targets. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth posted on X: 'Iran made a poor choice. Now they pay.' President Trump declared the ceasefire with Iran over but said mediated talks would continue. Qatari mediators visited Iran on Friday for de-escalation talks, and Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi travelled to Oman on Saturday for further discussions.

The closure follows a week in which three commercial tankers were attacked on a US-recommended route through Omani waters, exchanges of strikes between the US and Iran killed at least 17 people and wounded 115, and Iran's new Supreme Leader Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei — in his first public statement since taking power — called for vengeance for his father's assassination, calling it 'the will of the nation.' Trump warned that any assassination attempt on him would trigger the US to 'decimate and destroy all areas' of Iran.
sections.whyItMatters: The Hormuz closure is the most direct escalation since the US-Iran war began in February. One-fifth of global oil and LNG passes through the strait in peacetime. Iran is betting that calibrated military pressure combined with continued diplomacy gives it leverage over the MoU's implementation — but the gap between what Tehran calls a 'safe route' and what Washington accepts as free passage may be narrowing to a point of no return.

sections.heading: Iran and US Trade Narratives as Diplomacy Hangs On
sections.body:
Beneath the strikes, both sides are still talking — but through radically different lenses. US officials told American media that Iranian counterparts privately blamed the tanker attacks on a 'rogue' hardline faction trying to undermine negotiations. Iran's UN Ambassador Amir Saeid Iravani warned that any external interference in managing the strait would violate the MoU and delay restoring maritime traffic.

Iran has established a 'Persian Gulf Strait Authority' to control authorized transit, insisting ships use its designated route. The IMO's governing council on Friday 'strongly condemned' Iran's claim of sovereignty over the strait, calling on its 176 member states not to recognise any Iranian decisions aimed at 'closing, obstructing, hampering or otherwise interfering with international navigation.' The US Treasury imposed its first new Iran-related sanctions since the MoU, designating individuals including Ali Ansari, a financial facilitator linked to Mojtaba Khamenei.

Ali Vaez, the Crisis Group's Iran Project director, characterized the revenge rhetoric as domestic signalling while diplomacy aims to prevent another punishing round of war. Negar Mortazavi, a senior fellow at the Center for International Policy, said Iran 'has not closed the door to talks' but will use 'calibrated military pressure' to establish its interpretation of the MoU, believing that 'diplomacy without deterrence would give the US and Israel time to regroup and resume the war later.'
sections.whyItMatters: The gap between public rhetoric and private signalling is wide, but both sides are keeping the diplomatic channel open. The key question is whether the next escalation — or the next miscommunication — closes that gap permanently.

sections.heading: Spain Battles One of Its Deadliest Wildfires as Heatwave Grips Southern Europe
sections.body:
A wildfire in the Los Gallardos area of Almería, southeastern Spain, has killed at least 12 people — among them four British nationals, according to Spanish authorities — with another 23 people still missing. Andalusia's emergencies minister Antonio Sanz said the blaze has burned 6,600 hectares (16,300 acres). The cause has been attributed to a fallen power line, though local electricity companies have denied responsibility.

Sustained heatwave conditions with temperatures around 40°C (104°F) have fueled wildfires across Southern Europe this summer, including in France and Portugal. In France, more than 10,000 people were evacuated from the Pyrenees foothills on Monday, and Interior Minister Laurent Nuñez said 32 people had been arrested on suspicion of starting fires. The Copernicus climate service notes that Europe is the fastest-warming continent, heating twice as fast as the global average.

With at least 12 dead, the Los Gallardos fire is already among Spain's deadliest. The 1984 La Gomera fire killed 20, and the 1979 Lloret de Mar fire killed 21.
sections.whyItMatters: This is no longer an environmental story alone. The scale of civilian casualties — with foreign nationals among the dead — turns climate-driven wildfire into a cross-border political and public safety crisis for the EU.

sections.heading: Typhoon Bavi Forces Nearly Two Million From Homes in China
sections.body:
Typhoon Bavi made landfall on China's eastern coast on Saturday, the second major storm to hit the country in a week. The storm, spanning roughly 1,000 km at its widest — the width of France — came ashore near Taizhou before a second landfall near Wenzhou, a city of 10 million. More than 1.7 million people were evacuated in Zhejiang province alone, with schools, flights, and train services suspended.

Bavi began as a super typhoon with 290 km/h winds, battering Guam and the Northern Mariana Islands before weakening to 144 km/h. It struck Japan's Sakishima islands — injuring at least five people and knocking out power to thousands — and drenched northern Taiwan. Earlier, landslides triggered by the storm killed at least 17 in the Philippines.

The storm follows Typhoon Maysak earlier in the week, which left at least 39 dead in China and spawned two rare tornadoes in Hubei province. Parts of southern China are still reeling from Maysak's devastation when Bavi arrived.
sections.whyItMatters: Back-to-back super typhoons hitting the same densely populated coastline within a week is a pattern consistent with intensifying tropical cyclone seasons. The combined toll — evacuations, infrastructure damage, agricultural loss — strains regional resilience and raises questions about coastal preparedness across East Asia.

sections.heading: US Subpoenas NYT Reporters Over Air Force One Reporting
sections.body:
The Trump administration has issued federal grand jury subpoenas to several New York Times journalists over their reporting on security concerns surrounding the president's new Air Force One plane — a Boeing 747-8 gifted by Qatar valued at roughly $400 million. The Times reported that the Secret Service urged Trump to switch planes on his return from the NATO summit in Turkey, citing inadequate security features including antimissile capabilities.

Times newsroom lawyer David McCraw called the subpoenas a 'brazen act' designed to intimidate journalists and prevent the public from learning about government operations. The Justice Department said the investigation targets 'those leaking classified information, not reporters,' adding that 'reporters are not the targets.' The reporters are required to appear before a grand jury in Manhattan on Wednesday.

The move comes as the administration wages a simultaneous military escalation with Iran and faces mounting scrutiny over the Qatar-donated aircraft's security adequacy. Press freedom advocates and members of Congress have condemned the subpoenas as an attempt to threaten and intimidate.
sections.whyItMatters: A sitting administration using grand jury subpoenas against reporters covering national security is a significant escalation in the long-running tension between press freedom and government secrecy. The dual track — prosecuting leakers while pressuring the journalists who reported their information — signals a harder line on press accountability.

sections.heading: Lebanon-Israel Pilot Zone Talks Advance as Poland Confronts Ukraine Over WW2 Massacres
sections.body:
US and Lebanese military delegations met in Beirut on Saturday to discuss implementing the first phase of a US-brokered framework for Israeli withdrawal from southern Lebanon. The talks focus on establishing a mechanism for the first of two 'pilot zones' where the long-disempowered Lebanese military would take full security control. Hezbollah opposes the direct talks, preferring the broader US-Iran ceasefire framework. Israel has continued attacks in southern Lebanon despite the ceasefire, with seven people injured in al-Mansouri on Saturday and Israeli forces advancing into Beit Yahoun with tanks and bulldozers. More than a million people have been displaced; about 732,000 have returned home so far. The next round of Lebanon-Israel negotiations is scheduled for Rome on Wednesday and Thursday.

Separately, Polish Prime Minister Donald Tusk announced the creation of a national memorial to victims of a 'genocide committed by Ukrainian nationalists' during World War Two, marking the anniversary of the Volhynia massacres in which around 100,000 ethnic Poles were killed by the Ukrainian Insurgent Army. President Karol Nawrocki stressed the row would not affect Poland's support for Ukraine against Russia. President Zelensky acknowledged joint commemoration prayers and said Ukraine was working to 'honestly establish the facts,' but reminded that Russia remains the common 'mortal threat' to both nations. Three former Ukrainian presidents returned their Polish state awards in solidarity with Zelensky.
sections.whyItMatters: The Lebanon-Israel talks inch forward but remain fragile — Israel's continued strikes on Lebanese territory alongside diplomatic negotiations illustrate how far implementation lags behind agreement. In Poland-Ukraine relations, Tusk's memorial announcement tests the limits of wartime solidarity by pressing a historical grievance that resonates deeply in both countries at a moment when unity against Russia matters most.

bottomLine: Iran's closure of the Strait of Hormuz — the most direct escalation since the war began — turns a contested waterway into a controlled chokepoint, even as both sides keep diplomatic channels open through Qatar and Oman. Whether this is coercive bargaining or the beginning of a wider rupture depends on whether the next ship that tries to pass uses Iran's route or its own. Meanwhile, climate-driven crises on three continents — Spain's deadly wildfire, China's back-to-back typhoons, Uganda's drought — are producing casualties that rival armed conflicts, and the Trump administration's subpoena of NYT reporters signals that press pressure is now part of the governing playbook. The Lebanese-Israeli pilot zone talks and Poland's Volhynia memorial are reminders that even amid war, the slow machinery of historical reckoning and post-conflict arrangement keeps grinding.
